name: Discovery Verification and Proof of Service Audit
summary: This skill produces an attorney-grade audit memorandum assessing whether written discovery responses are legally binding and timely by verifying signature authority, party verification formalities (including under-penalty-of-perjury compliance), proof of service sufficiency, and deadline computation with mailbox-rule extensions. It integrates adversarial risk analysis across federal and state forums, emphasizes documentary support for every factual assertion, and embeds anti-hallucination guardrails requiring web-verified citations or explicit flags for attorney confirmation. The output is designed to support cures, meet-and-confer strategy, and motion-readiness, subject to mandatory attorney review.
content:
# Discovery Verification and Proof of Service Audit

## 1. Opening Context

This skill produces a structured audit memorandum assessing whether written discovery responses are legally operative and defensible. In most U.S. jurisdictions, interrogatory answers and certain other discovery responses must be verified—often under oath or under penalty of perjury—by the party, not merely signed by counsel. Service must likewise be provable and timely under the applicable procedural rules. A discovery response without a valid verification is, in many jurisdictions, effectively a legal nullity: it cannot support or oppose a motion for summary judgment, may be treated as a failure to respond entirely, and can trigger waiver of objections. Similarly, a flawed proof of service can defeat timeliness arguments, expire jurisdictional clocks for motions to compel, and undermine credibility with the court.

This audit prevents the two most avoidable categories of discovery failure: (i) responses that can be attacked as unverified, unsigned, or not attributable to the client, and (ii) responses that are untimely because deadlines were miscalculated, extensions were misapplied, or service proof is defective. Good execution looks like lawyer-grade diligence: identifying the governing signature and verification rules, confirming that the correct person signed in the correct form, validating perjury-language compliance, calculating deadlines under the correct service and time-computation framework, and producing an audit record that would withstand scrutiny from opposing counsel and the court.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

Written discovery responses are not informal communications; they are representations attributable to the party and, depending on the jurisdiction and document type, sworn statements usable as admissions, impeachment material, and evidentiary building blocks at summary judgment or trial. The verification (or declaration under penalty of perjury) is the bridge between an unsworn document and admissible evidence. Without it, a response is merely the lawyer's narrative—not evidence a court can rely upon. The proof of service provides the paper trail from which all subsequent deadlines are calculated. Together, verification and proof of service are the "authentication layer" that makes discovery responses enforceable and usable.

This skill fits into the matter lifecycle at two common inflection points: immediately before serving discovery responses (pre-service quality assurance), and after receiving an opponent's responses (to identify defects for meet-and-confer, motion-to-compel, or waiver-preservation purposes). The primary audiences are the supervising attorney, litigation support staff, and—in contested settings—the judge or discovery referee who may rely on the audit to resolve a timeliness or waiver dispute.

Strategically, the audit serves three goals. First, it protects the client from sanctions and waiver by ensuring responses are properly executed and served. Second, it preserves offensive leverage by identifying defects in an opponent's responses early, so counsel can demand cures and build a record for cost-shifting. Third, it reduces disputes by documenting the "what, when, and how" of service and execution in a way that is easy to explain and prove later. In an adversarial context, opposing counsel may attempt to "pocket-serve" documents or use ambiguous service methods to shorten response time; auditing the proof of service ensures that the client's rights are protected and that the litigation remains on a fair, predictable schedule.

Scope boundaries: this skill focuses on signature and verification sufficiency and service and deadline mechanics for written discovery responses and related documents (interrogatories, requests for production, requests for admission, and similar written responses). It does not replace substantive objection drafting, privilege-log strategy, ESI protocol negotiation, or motion practice briefing—though it should flag when defects create motion leverage or waiver risk.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before performing the audit, the executing LLM must obtain the exact documents and transmission facts that determine whether responses are binding and timely. The audit cannot be reliably performed from summaries. The LLM should ask for missing inputs and pause until it has them, explicitly prompting: "Do you have the Proof of Service and the Verification page for this discovery? I cannot calculate deadlines or assess validity without them."

At minimum, collect the following categories of materials. First, the discovery set and the response set as actually served, including all attachments and signature pages: the propounding discovery requests (interrogatories, requests for production, requests for admission) with any definitions, instructions, and service page; the responding party's written responses as served, including verifications, signature blocks, and any exhibit lists; any stipulations, emails, or court orders modifying response deadlines; and the proof(s) of service for both the requests and the responses (or other evidence of service method and date/time, such as e-filing notices, certified mail receipts, courier confirmations, or email headers if service by email is authorized). Where available, obtain scans of postmarks or electronic service headers to verify the assertions in the proof of service.

Second, collect jurisdiction and forum details because verification and deadline rules are highly forum-specific: the jurisdiction (state and county, or federal district); whether the case is in state court or federal court; the governing procedural rules identified by counsel (e.g., Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, California Code of Civil Procedure); and any applicable local rules or standing orders that affect service or signature requirements. Third, the master service list—the list of all parties and counsel of record—to ensure the proof of service includes all necessary recipients.

Fourth, obtain the date facts needed to compute deadlines: the date the discovery requests were served; the method(s) of service used; the date the responses were served; the method(s) of service used for responses; and whether any party is outside the jurisdiction or service was international. Also obtain the court's holiday schedule and a reliable calendar for counting purposes.

Finally, gather party identity and capacity information relevant to who may sign: whether the responding party is an individual, corporation, partnership, LLC, governmental entity, or unincorporated association; whether any representative is signing (officer, managing agent, custodian, litigation representative); the identity and title of the signer; and whether the responses are verified in a format compatible with the forum's perjury statute.

If any required item is missing—especially the verification page(s), proof of service, or any deadline-modifying stipulation or order—the LLM must request the missing documents or facts before reaching conclusions.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Confirm the Governing Rule Set and Classify the Discovery Package

Begin by locking down the governing procedural regime, because the audit turns on whether the forum treats certain defects as curable technicalities or as fatal non-responses with waiver consequences. Identify whether the matter is in federal court under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, or in a specific state court with its own discovery statutes. If the user does not specify, infer cautiously from captions and case numbers, but flag the inference for attorney confirmation.

Next, classify exactly what document types are being audited. Interrogatory responses are the highest-risk category for verification defects. Some forums also require verification for responses to requests for admission, and certain jurisdictions require declarations for document production responses. Identify whether the package includes: responses only; responses plus document production; responses with supplemental responses; amended responses; or objections-only responses.

Then identify whether the audit is for outgoing service (pre-service quality assurance) or incoming service (opponent responses). The posture changes the recommended remediation: for outgoing service, defects can be fixed before they create waiver or sanctions risk; for incoming service, the focus shifts to preserving objections, waiver arguments, and motion deadlines.

The audit memo's introduction should frame its scope clearly. For example: "Counsel requested an audit of Defendant's Responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Interrogatories and Requests for Production to confirm (1) whether the responses are properly executed and verified by an authorized party representative under the governing rules, and (2) whether service of the responses was timely and provable under applicable service and time-computation rules. This audit is limited to signature/verification and service/deadline mechanics and does not address substantive adequacy of responses."

Common mistakes at this step include assuming federal signature standards apply in state court, treating "signed by counsel" as equivalent to "verified by the party," and failing to separate the deadline analysis for each discovery instrument when the jurisdiction uses different response periods for interrogatories, requests for production, and requests for admission. The LLM must explicitly identify the rule source and include a citation plan with verification instructions. If the LLM cannot verify the rule source via web research, it must flag the citation for attorney confirmation.

### Step 2: Audit Verification for Formal Sufficiency

The core analysis begins with examining the verification itself. A common mistake is assuming that any signature is sufficient. In most jurisdictions, the verification must use specific language to be effective. Under 28 U.S.C. § 1746, which governs unsworn declarations in federal practice, the signer must state that they declare under penalty of perjury "under the laws of the United States of America" that the foregoing is true and correct (https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/28/1746). In California, CCP § 2015.5 requires that the declaration state it is made under penalty of perjury "under the laws of the State of California" and include the location and date of signing; if signed outside of California, it must either specify that it is under California law or take the form of a notarized affidavit.

First, confirm whether verification is required for the specific discovery type in the governing forum. In federal practice, interrogatories must be answered by the party under oath (FRCP 33(b)(3)), while objections are signed by the attorney (FRCP 33(b)(5)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_33). Requests for admission are answered or objected to by the party or its attorney (FRCP 36(a)(3)–(4)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_36). Requests for production responses are signed by the party or its attorney (FRCP 34(b)(2)(A)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_34). State courts may impose additional verification requirements for RFAs or RFPs; those must be verified via jurisdiction-specific research and flagged for attorney confirmation if not confirmed.

Next, check whether the verification includes all necessary elements: an affirmation that the responses are true and correct; language placing the statement under oath or "under penalty of perjury"; the correct jurisdictional reference in the perjury clause (e.g., "under the laws of the United States" or "under the laws of the State of [X]"); a signature by the declarant; and a date. Then confirm that the verification is properly attached and clearly references the specific responses it verifies. Watch for "floating verifications" that do not identify the discovery set (e.g., "I have read the foregoing document" without naming the interrogatories) or where the responses are not actually attached. In adversarial practice, opponents argue that such verifications do not cover the served responses.

Also check for internal consistency: the declarant's name should match the signature block; dates should be plausible (e.g., a verification dated after the proof of service date is a major red flag indicating the proof of service was signed before the verification was ready); and the verification should not refer to a different case or party.

As illustrative cure language (to be adapted to the governing jurisdiction after attorney review): "I, [Name], am [title/relationship] of [Party]. I have read the foregoing Responses to [Propounding Party]'s [Set Name] Interrogatories and know the contents thereof. The responses are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief, except as to those matters stated on information and belief, and as to those matters I believe them to be true. I declare under penalty of perjury under the laws of [State/United States] that the foregoing is true and correct. Executed on [date] at [city, state]."

Common mistakes include omitting "under penalty of perjury" where required; using the wrong jurisdiction in the perjury clause; failing to date the verification; having counsel sign the verification where only the party may do so; and using an electronic signature format not accepted for declarations in the relevant court.

### Step 3: Analyze Signature Authority and Capacity

Determining whether the correct person signed the verification requires analysis of the responding party's identity and the signer's authority. For individuals, the signer must be the individual party unless an exception applies (e.g., guardian, conservator, authorized agent). If someone else signs, the audit should flag that as high risk and require attorney review.

For entities (corporations, LLCs, partnerships), the signer must be an officer, managing agent, or other authorized representative with knowledge of the facts. The audit should capture the signer's title and basis for authority as stated or, if missing, flag the omission. A vague designation like "Authorized Representative" should be questioned, because opponents may challenge the verification as not executed by a proper person. If the responding party has identified individuals with knowledge through a Rule 30(b)(6) deposition notice or through interrogatory responses about corporate structure, cross-reference the signer against those individuals to ensure consistency.

A proper entity verification typically reads: "I, Jane Doe, am the Vice President of Operations for [Defendant Corp]. I have read the foregoing Responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Interrogatories and know the contents thereof. The same are true of my own knowledge, except as to those matters which are therein stated on information and belief, and as to those matters I believe them to be true."

For government entities or institutional parties, ensure the signing individual is an authorized official and that the verification language is consistent with the forum's accepted practice.

A critical pitfall involves attorney verifications. In many jurisdictions, an attorney verification is only permitted in narrow circumstances—for example, in California, only when the party is absent from the county where the attorney's office is located, and even then the attorney's verification may waive attorney-client privilege as to the sources of information. An improper attorney verification should be treated as a total failure to verify. The audit should always confirm that the party—not just counsel—has signed the verification for any discovery instrument that requires party verification.

Common mistakes include relying solely on counsel's signature, missing that the verification is attached but unsigned, assuming an employee title implies authority without documenting it, and failing to confirm whether the responses include multiple discovery instruments but only one verification covering them all.

Adversarial awareness: in a motion to compel or sanctions context, opponents will argue that an unauthorized verification is equivalent to no verification and that objections are waived. The audit should explicitly connect any authority defects to potential consequences and propose a cure plan.

### Step 4: Audit Proof of Service Sufficiency and Service Method Validity

A timely response is only as defensible as the ability to prove service. Start by identifying whether the forum requires a proof or certificate of service for discovery responses and what form it must take. In federal practice, proof of service is generally required for papers served under FRCP 5, and service methods include personal service, mail, leaving at the person's office, electronic means with consent, and other methods specified in the rule (FRCP 5(b)(2)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_5). Discovery responses are often not filed unless used in the proceeding or required by local rule or order; the audit should distinguish "service sufficiency" from "filing compliance."

Examine the proof or certificate of service included with the response package. Confirm it states: the date of service; the method of service; the name and address (or electronic service address) of each recipient; and the server's identity and signature (and, where required, declaration under penalty of perjury). If multiple methods were used (e.g., email plus mail), ensure the proof explains this clearly.

Critically, confirm the service method was authorized. Email or electronic service may require written consent, stipulation, ECF registration, or a court order depending on the forum. If the proof of service states "served via email" but there is no stipulation or rule basis, flag this as potentially invalid service that affects deadline computation.

Cross-reference the recipient list against the master service list to ensure all parties were served. In multi-party litigation, a failure to serve one party can be used as a tactic to delay proceedings or to claim that a party was not on notice of a discovery admission. If you are the recipient, check your own address on the proof of service; if the address is wrong, even if you received the document, the service might be technically invalid—a powerful leverage point if the opposing party is trying to enforce a deadline.

When auditing opponent service, also look for red flags suggesting service gamesmanship: service late at night, ambiguous time zones, partial service (responses served but verification mailed later), service to an outdated address, or use of an old service list that misses a new party.

As an illustrative audit finding: "The Proof of Service states service on 01/15/2026 by email to plaintiff's counsel at [email] and by U.S. Mail to [address]. The case file should be checked for (a) a written stipulation permitting email service, or (b) a court rule or standing order authorizing electronic service between counsel. Absent authorization, email service may be ineffective, and the operative service date may be the mailing date. Counsel should verify under the governing rules."

Common mistakes include assuming an email constitutes valid service in every forum; failing to include all recipients; listing the wrong address; omitting the service date; and failing to have the proof signed by a competent person.

### Step 5: Calculate Response Deadlines Using Correct Time-Counting Rules, Including Mailbox Rule Extensions

Deadline computation is the other high-risk area. The audit must calculate the baseline response period, the trigger date (date of service), the day-counting methodology (calendar days versus court days; inclusion or exclusion of weekends and holidays), and any extensions based on service method (the "mailbox rule"), plus any stipulations or court-ordered modifications.

In federal practice, start with the discovery-specific response period: interrogatories are due within 30 days after being served (FRCP 33(b)(2)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_33); requests for production are due within 30 days (FRCP 34(b)(2)(A)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_34); and requests for admission are due within 30 days (FRCP 36(a)(3)) (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_36). Then apply the federal time-counting rule (FRCP 6(a)): exclude the day of the triggering event and count every subsequent day, including intermediate weekends and legal holidays; if the period ends on a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, it runs to the next business day (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_6).

Next, consider whether additional time is added for certain service methods. FRCP 6(d) adds 3 days when a party must act within a specified time after being served and service is made by mail, leaving with the clerk's office, or other specified methods (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_6). Importantly, after the 2016 amendments to the Federal Rules, the 3-day extension no longer applies to service made by electronic means under FRCP 5(b)(2)(E). The audit should explicitly quote or reference the current rule text and state whether the trigger is "after service" (generally yes for discovery response periods) and whether the specific method of service qualifies for the extension.

In state courts, mailbox rule extensions vary significantly. In California, for example, CCP § 1013 adds 5 calendar days for mail within California, 10 calendar days for mail from outside California, 2 court days for electronic service (under CCP § 1010.6), and 2 calendar days for overnight delivery (https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=1013.&lawCode=CCP). A critical distinction is between "calendar days" and "court days": the electronic service extension of 2 court days in California can span a long weekend (e.g., Friday to Wednesday if Monday is a holiday). The LLM must perform jurisdiction-specific research for the mailbox rule and cite the governing statute or rule with a URL, or flag the computation for attorney confirmation if the rule cannot be verified.

The audit should present deadline computations transparently, showing each step of the calculation and the assumptions used. This is essential because judges and opposing counsel will check the math. As an illustrative calculation in federal practice: "Plaintiff served Interrogatories by mail on January 2, 2026. Under FRCP 33(b)(2), responses are due 30 days after service. Under FRCP 6(a), the day of service (January 2) is excluded and counting begins January 3. Because service was by mail, FRCP 6(d) adds 3 days. The 30-day period ends February 1, 2026 (a Sunday), which rolls to February 2 (Monday). Adding 3 days for mail under FRCP 6(d) produces February 5, 2026. Counsel should confirm the holiday schedule and whether any stipulation modifies this deadline."

Common mistakes include counting from the wrong trigger (mailing date versus receipt date versus e-service timestamp); applying a mailbox extension when service was personal or via ECF (where the 3-day extension does not apply in federal court after 2016); ignoring stipulations; ignoring holiday and weekend rollovers; confusing calendar days with court days; and misapplying different response periods for different discovery devices.

Adversarial awareness: timeliness disputes often become waiver disputes. In California, the 45-day deadline to move to compel further responses to interrogatories is jurisdictional under CCP § 2030.300—if counsel miscalculates the service extension and files on day 46, the motion may be denied and the client may lose all rights to that discovery. The audit should connect computed dates to risk: if responses were late, identify whether objections may be waived under the governing law, and recommend immediate remedial steps (seek stipulation, serve amended responses, move for protective order if needed).

### Step 6: Identify Defects, Assess Consequences, and Recommend Cure Strategy

After identifying defects, the audit must translate them into practical consequences and cure options. Defects should be categorized by severity. Fatal defects include a missing signature, an attorney verification where only a party verification is permitted, absence of "penalty of perjury" language where required, and complete failure to serve one or more parties. Technical defects include an incorrect address on the proof of service, a missing city or state of signing, miscalculated extension days, or a floating verification that does not identify the specific discovery set.

For verification defects, assess whether the defect likely renders the response entirely ineffective, partially effective (e.g., objections signed by counsel may stand while answers under oath are missing), or curable by serving a corrected verification. In federal court, FRCP 26(g) requires counsel to sign discovery responses and certifies reasonable inquiry, and sanctions may follow for violations (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_26). This is separate from the party verification requirement for interrogatory answers under FRCP 33(b)(3). The audit should explain this distinction so the supervising attorney can decide whether to cure, compel, or seek sanctions. In California, an unverified interrogatory response is treated as no response at all for purposes of the motion to compel clock, as illustrated by the holding in *Appleton v. Superior Court* (1988) 206 Cal.App.3d 632.

For service defects, assess whether service can be proven and whether an alternative effective service date is likely (e.g., mail date rather than email date where email was unauthorized). For timeliness defects, consider whether there was a stipulation, whether the opponent has waived timeliness arguments by conduct, and whether there is still time to negotiate an extension.

The output should include recommended next steps framed as options for attorney decision: serving an amended verification page; serving corrected responses; requesting a stipulation confirming the service date and extending deadlines; sending a meet-and-confer letter identifying defects and demanding a cure by a date certain; or preparing a motion to compel or for sanctions. Under the duty to meet and confer (e.g., CCP § 2030.300 in California or the general requirement under FRCP 37(a)(1) to certify that counsel conferred or attempted to confer before filing a motion to compel), counsel cannot simply file a motion based on a missing verification without first writing to opposing counsel and requesting a cure. The LLM must not advise the user to file a motion or threaten sanctions as a definitive step; it should present these as potential strategies for attorney evaluation.

As illustrative cure recommendation language: "Because the interrogatory answers are not verified by the party, opposing counsel could argue the responses are not compliant and seek an order compelling verified answers along with fees. A low-cost cure is to serve a corrected verification executed by an authorized representative, expressly referencing the served responses, and to confirm in writing that the substantive responses are unchanged. Counsel should evaluate whether any objections are at risk of waiver due to lateness under the governing jurisdiction's standards."

Common mistakes include declaring waiver or sanctions as automatic without researching the forum's standard; "fixing" substantive answers without tracking them as amended or supplemental; and failing to preserve the record (e.g., not saving envelope postmarks, ECF notifications, or email headers). The audit should recommend documenting the timeline, identifying what was served when, and ensuring any cure is complete and traceable.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

This skill produces litigation work product that can affect client rights, court deadlines, and sanction exposure. Competent execution requires attention to professional responsibility and candor constraints.

The Model Rules of Professional Conduct implicated include Rule 1.1 (Competence), requiring sufficient legal knowledge and thoroughness in identifying signature, verification, and deadline rules—failing to audit a verification is a breach of this duty if it leads to the loss of evidence; Rule 1.3 (Diligence), requiring timely action to cure defects and meet deadlines; Rule 1.4 (Communication), requiring counsel to keep the client informed about verification obligations and the need for a party signature; Rule 1.6 (Confidentiality), requiring the audit to avoid disclosing sensitive content and to treat discovery materials as confidential; Rule 3.3 (Candor Toward the Tribunal), relevant if the audit's results are later used in declarations or motions—dates and service representations must be accurate, and submitting a discovery response known to be improperly verified to a court violates this duty; Rule 4.1 (Truthfulness in Statements to Others), relevant to meet-and-confer letters about service and timeliness; and Rule 5.3 (Responsibilities Regarding Nonlawyer Assistance), relevant because an LLM-assisted process must be supervised and its outputs verified by an attorney.

Key malpractice risk areas include miscalculating deadlines due to incorrect application of mailbox rules or holiday counting; missing that interrogatory answers require party verification under oath and serving only attorney-signed responses; assuming email service is valid without consent or authorization; failing to preserve proof of service evidence; and making overbroad waiver assertions without confirming jurisdictional law. The most common malpractice trap in this area is the motion-to-compel clock: in California, the 45-day clock under CCP § 2030.300 is jurisdictional, meaning a miscalculated service extension that results in a filing on day 46 may permanently forfeit the client's right to that discovery.

Privilege and confidentiality considerations also arise. If an attorney verifies a discovery response where only a party verification is proper, the attorney may be designated as a witness regarding the source of the facts, which can lead to waiver of attorney-client privilege or work-product protection. Discovery responses may also contain privileged communications, attorney mental impressions, or sensitive personal, health, or financial data. The LLM should minimize quoting substantive discovery content in the audit and instead reference page or paragraph numbers. If documents include protective-order material, the audit should be labeled and handled consistently with that order.

Candor and accuracy are paramount: if the audit will support any filing, every factual statement about service date, method, or signature must be backed by the proof of service, ECF notice, email headers, or other documentary evidence. If the evidence is ambiguous (e.g., missing proof of service), the audit must state the uncertainty and recommend obtaining confirmation.

Statutory and compliance overlays may apply in certain matters (e.g., regulated industries, HIPAA-protected data, consumer privacy statutes). This skill does not provide regulatory compliance advice; it should flag where discovery contains regulated data and advise attorney review of any protective-order and data-handling obligations.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

A high-quality audit reads like something that could be attached to an internal case status email or used to draft a meet-and-confer letter with minimal rework. Review the output across four categories: legal correctness, factual support, completeness, and litigation utility.

Legal correctness means the audit accurately states the governing rule sources and does not overclaim. Every statement about what the rules "require" should be supported by a verified citation URL (e.g., Cornell LII for federal rules, the official state legislative site for state statutes) or flagged for attorney confirmation. The most common unacceptable failure is hallucinated statutes or cases. If the LLM cannot verify a jurisdiction-specific mailbox rule or verification statute via web research, it must not invent it; it must describe the issue, identify the likely rule category, and request attorney confirmation. Any citation to specific extension days (e.g., "plus 5 days for mail") must be checked against the most current version of the applicable code before being stated as authoritative.

Factual support means each conclusion ties back to an exhibit: the signature page, verification page, proof of service, ECF notice, or stipulation/order. The audit should identify where in the documents the relevant fact appears (Bates range or PDF page number) and should avoid relying on assumptions about what "must have happened."

Completeness means the audit addresses each discovery device; each required signature and verification; the validity of the service method; deadline computation with any extensions; and consequences and cure options.

Litigation utility means the audit produces a clean bottom line that a supervising attorney can act on: whether responses are binding, whether they are timely, what defects exist, and how to cure or exploit them. The output should include a summary report addressing verification status (signed, by the party, with the perjury clause), date validation (whether the verification date is consistent with the proof-of-service date), service calculation (date of service, method, specific code section for the extension, and final calculated deadline), and adversarial risk (whether the opposing party's defects provide a strategic advantage or pose a risk to the client).

All output from this skill requires attorney review before use in client communications, meet-and-confer correspondence, declarations, or court filings. An attorney must personally verify the presence of the signature and the final deadline calculation before it is calendared. This skill does not constitute legal advice and is intended to support attorney-supervised legal work.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

This skill is designed to operate across U.S. jurisdictions, but signature and verification requirements and mailbox rule extensions diverge materially.

In federal court, the audit should treat FRCP 33(b)(3) as the baseline for interrogatory verification ("answered by the party to whom they are directed … under oath") and counsel signature requirements under FRCP 33(b)(5) and FRCP 26(g) as separate layers of compliance (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_33; https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_26). While interrogatories require a party verification, requests for production under Rule 34 and requests for admission under Rule 36 do not always require a separate party verification in the same way, though the attorney's signature under Rule 26(g) carries its own certification obligations. Time computation should follow FRCP 6(a) and any service-method additions under FRCP 6(d), but the audit must confirm whether FRCP 6(d) applies to the specific service method used and must account for the 2016 amendment eliminating the 3-day extension for electronic service. Local rules or ECF procedures may further affect timing (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_6).

In California, the requirements are stricter and more detailed. CCP § 2030.250 and § 2031.250 require party verifications for interrogatory and document production responses, respectively, and without them the response is "unverified" and does not constitute a response at all for purposes of the motion to compel clock (*Appleton v. Superior Court* (1988) 206 Cal.App.3d 632). CCP § 2015.5 governs the form of declarations under penalty of perjury and includes location-of-signing requirements. Mailbox rule extensions under CCP § 1013 add 5 calendar days for mail within California, 10 calendar days for mail from outside California, 2 calendar days for overnight delivery, and 2 court days for electronic service under CCP § 1010.6 (https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=1013.&lawCode=CCP). The distinction between court days and calendar days is critical and frequently causes errors.

In New York, under CPLR 3020, verifications are required for certain pleadings and discovery. New York practice permits attorney verifications more broadly than California or federal practice, but the specific circumstances under which an attorney verification is acceptable and any resulting privilege implications must be confirmed through jurisdiction-specific research.

For other high-volume forums such as Texas, Florida, and Illinois, do not rely on generalized assumptions for specific extension days or verification statutes; those are common hallucination traps. Instead, use web research to obtain the exact statutory language and cite it with a URL, or flag the requirement for attorney confirmation.

Where the forum uses court days (business days) versus calendar days, or has special holiday rules, the audit must follow that jurisdiction's computation rule and explicitly identify which method it applied. If any party is outside the U.S. or service implicates the Hague Service Convention or foreign blocking statutes, the skill should flag that the audit is not sufficient on its own and requires attorney review of international service and data transfer obligations.
